title: Stage One Purpose at a Time
summary: Separate supplied community-site edits so each commit records one understandable project purpose.

sketchBlocks.title: Stage only the changes that belong together
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: You edited the community-site to fix a wording mistake on the homepage and also tweaked a color in the stylesheet. These are two different purposes: the wording fix belongs in a "copy" commit and the color tweak belongs in a "style" commit. Staging only the file(s) that correspond to one purpose keeps each commit focused and meaningful.

Example workspace files (edited, unstaged):

- community-site/index.html — homepage copy corrected
- community-site/styles.css — temporary color tweak

How you check and commit a single purpose:

1. Inspect the unstaged changes with git status and git diff to confirm what changed.
2. Stage only the file for the copy fix with git add community-site/index.html.
3. Confirm the staged snapshot with git diff --staged so you know exactly what will go into the commit.
4. Commit with a concise message describing the single purpose, for example:

```
git add community-site/index.html
# then inspect
git diff --staged
# then commit the copy fix
git commit -m "Fix homepage typo"
```

Why this matters: When only one purpose is recorded per commit, the history is readable. A later reader can open a commit with git show and understand its intent without wading through unrelated style changes.

Step by step, read the commands in the order shown. The first line runs `git add community-site/index.html` as the next repository step in the example. The second line runs `git diff --staged` as the next repository step in the example. The third line runs `git commit -m "Fix homepage typo"` as the next repository step in the example. Together, the lines form one complete example rather than unrelated commands.

sketchBlocks.title: See what staging captured before committing
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: The staging area holds a snapshot of content you plan to commit. It can differ from the working tree if you staged only some files. Before committing, use git diff --staged to inspect exactly what the next commit will record.

Concrete steps:

1. Make edits across multiple files for different purposes (for example, styles and a contributor note).
2. Stage only the style change with git add community-site/styles.css.
3. Run git diff --staged. The output shows only the staged file's changes — what the upcoming commit will include — not the unstaged contributor note.

Example commands (do not copy verbatim into a Try It; use them as an illustration):

```
git add community-site/styles.css
git diff --staged
# verify the staged snapshot shows the style change but not other edits
git commit -m "Refine header color"
```

Interpretation: If git diff --staged shows only one file, you can be confident the commit will remain purpose-focused.

sketchBlocks.title: Stage, inspect, commit, then leave unrelated work alone
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Suppose you added a short CONTRIBUTING note and also updated the homepage copy in separate files. To keep commits focused, stage only the CONTRIBUTING change and leave the homepage edits unstaged. After committing, use git status and git diff to verify the remaining work is still unstaged and not part of the commit.

Step-by-step:

- Stage the file(s) for the purpose: git add community-site/CONTRIB.txt
- Inspect staged snapshot: git diff --staged
- Commit with a purposeful message: git commit -m "Add contributor guidelines"
- Confirm the working tree still contains the other edits: git status and git diff

A single-commit mistake to avoid: staging all modified files by accident (git add .) which mixes unrelated purposes into one commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Use log and show to inspect what you just recorded
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After making a focused commit, use git log --oneline to read the concise history and git show <commit> (or git show HEAD) to inspect the committed snapshot. Reading the commit message plus the staged snapshot verifies the commit really captured the intended purpose.

Example flow:

1. git log --oneline to see the recent commit(s) with their messages.
2. git show HEAD to view the changes recorded by the latest commit.

Sample commands (illustrative):

```
git log --oneline -n 5
git show HEAD
```

What to check in the show output: file names changed, the hunk content that was staged, and that unrelated edits are absent. This confirms you created a focused, inspectable change.

quizDraft.title: Which command shows the staged snapshot that will be committed?
quizDraft.prompt: Which git command displays the exact snapshot of currently staged changes that will be recorded by the next commit?
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.title: What are correct descriptions of the staging area?
quizDraft.prompt: Select all statements that correctly describe the Git staging area (index).
options: It records a snapshot of files that will be included in the next commit.
It permanently stores uncommitted local changes outside of commits.
It automatically commits files when you stage them.
It prepares the exact content that will be recorded by the next commit.
quizDraft.hint: Choose the statements that describe what the index does in Git's commit workflow.

help.concept: The staging area holds snapshots of file content to be recorded by the next commit; it prepares changes and lets you review what will be included.
help.hint_1: The index is not the working tree; it's the snapshot you assemble for the next commit.
help.hint_2: Pick the statements that say it records snapshots and prepares content for commits, not statements that claim it auto-commits or permanently stores uncommitted changes.

quizDraft.title: Which commands help you read a recent commit and its recorded changes?
quizDraft.prompt: Choose the commands that show commit history and details about a single commit.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps for making a focused commit
quizDraft.prompt: Reorder the actions into the correct sequence for making a focused commit that records only one purpose.
tokens: Run git status and git diff to see unstaged edits
git add only the files for the single purpose
git diff --staged to inspect the staged snapshot
git commit -m "concise message describing the one purpose"
Use git status or git diff to verify unrelated edits remain unstaged
correctOrder: Run git status and git diff to see unstaged edits
git add only the files for the single purpose
git diff --staged to inspect the staged snapshot
git commit -m "concise message describing the one purpose"
Use git status or git diff to verify unrelated edits remain unstaged
quizDraft.hint: Think about inspect → stage → inspect snapshot → commit order.

help.concept: A focused commit workflow inspects changes, stages only related files, verifies the staged snapshot, and then commits with an appropriate message.
help.hint_1: Start by checking the current changes, not by staging everything immediately.
help.hint_2: After staging, use git diff --staged to confirm before committing.

quizDraft.title: Command to view staged snapshot
quizDraft.prompt: To view exactly what will be committed, run git [blank1].
quizDraft.template: To view exactly what will be committed, run git [blank1].
quizDraft.hint: This command compares the staging area to HEAD.

help.concept: git the missing term shows the content recorded in the index compared to HEAD; it reveals the staged snapshot for the next commit.
help.hint_1: The missing piece is the diff option that refers to staged content.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that mentions staged content explicitly.

quizDraft.title: What a focused commit should represent
quizDraft.prompt: A focused commit should contain changes for a single [blank1].
quizDraft.template: A focused commit should contain changes for a single [blank1].
quizDraft.hint: Think about the intent behind the changes, not a filename or a branch.

help.concept: A focused commit groups changes that serve one the missing term or intent (for example: 'fix typo', 'update docs', 'style cleanup'), making history easier to read.
help.hint_1: Pick the answer that refers to the reason for the change.
help.hint_2: The right word is about the intention or goal behind edits.

quizDraft.title: Stage only the copy fix and commit it
quizDraft.prompt:
You have a community-site repository with two edited files: index.html (homepage copy corrected) and styles.css (color tweak). Stage only the copy fix file and create a single focused commit with the message: Fix homepage typo

Type the commands in the terminal and press Enter for each command. The grader will verify that the commit recorded the index.html change and that styles.css remains unstaged in the working tree.

git diff --staged
git commit -m "Fix homepage typo"

Type the commands in the terminal and press Enter for each command. The grader will verify that the commit recorded the index.html change and that styles.css remains unstaged in the working tree.

quizDraft.title: Stage the style tweak and inspect the staged snapshot
quizDraft.prompt:
In a fresh community-site workspace you edited styles.css (header color refined) and added an unrelated contributor note in CONTRIB.txt. Stage only the style file, use git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot, then commit with the message: Refine header color

Type each command and press Enter. The grader will check that the commit recorded styles.css and that CONTRIB.txt is still unstaged.

quizDraft.title: Make a focused documentation commit and verify
quizDraft.prompt:
In a community-site workspace you updated README.md with a short note and also corrected a line in index.html. Stage only README.md and commit with the message: Update README with contributing link

Type each command and press Enter. After the commit, use git log --oneline and git show HEAD to confirm the commit message and view the exact recorded snapshot. The grader checks the commit message and that index.html edits remain unstaged.
